smell: as to their virtues, they are opening and attenuating.

56. Musci, mosses. These plants resemble the pines, firs, and other evergreens of that class, in the form and disposition of their leaves, and manner of growth of the female flowers, which are generally formed into a cone. They frequently creep, and extend themselves like a carpet upon the ground, trees, and stones, being generally collected into bunches and tufts: the smallest are only one-third of an inch in height, and the largest do not exceed five or six. Few of the mosses are annual: small as they are, the greater number are perennial and evergreens. Their growth is remarkably slow, as may be judged by the time that the antheræ take to ripen. This, reckoning from the first appearance of the antheræ to the dispersion of its powder or male dust, is generally four or six months. Although preserved dry for several years, these plants have the singular property of resuming their original verdure upon being moistened. It would be worth while to determine whether they do not also resume their vegetative quality. The roots of plants of this order are fibrous, slender, branched, and short. The stems are cylindric and weak, as are also the branches; they creep upon the ground, and strike root on every side. The leaves are very small and undivided. They differ with respect to situation; being either alternate, opposite, or placed by fours round the stalk. They have no perceptible footstalk nor middle rib, and are seated immediately upon the stems. The flowers are universally male and female: in some, the male flowers are produced upon the same plants with the female, and stand before them; in others, they are produced sometimes on the same, and sometimes on distinct plants. The male flowers consist entirely of antheræ, and their covering; proceed either singly, or in clusters, from the extremity of the branches, or angles of the leaves; and are either seated immediately upon the branches, or supported by a long footstalk. The female flowers, which generally resemble capsules or cones, are all placed immediately upon the stem or branches, without any footstalk; and proceed singly either from the wings of the leaves, or summit of the branches; when produced upon the same plant with the male they are always placed under them. The female cones of the mosses greatly resemble those of the pines and evergreen trees of that class; the scales which form them are true leaves, each containing in its wing or angle a single seed. When the seeds are ripe, the cones probably open for their dispersion. When shut, they resemble buds, and have sometimes been ignorantly mistaken for such. The calyx, in this order, if it can be called such, is that appearance resembling a vail or monk's cawl, which in the male flower covers or is suspended over the tops of the stamens like an extinguisher, and is termed by Linnaeus calyptra. The petals are universally wanting. The mosses in general are almost tasteless, have few juices, and being once dried do not readily imbibe moisture from the air. Those which grow in water, being thrown into the fire, grow red, and are reduced to ashes without receiving or communicating any flame; on which account some superstitious people, the Siberians in particular, place water moss in their chimnies as a preservative against fire. Most of the mosses are purgative; some violently so, and even emetic. They are all of wonderful efficacy in pre-

serving dry such bodies as are susceptible of moisture; and in retaining, for a long time, the humidity of young plants without exposing them to putrefaction. For this reason, such plants as are to be sent to any considerable distance, are generally wrapped up in them.

57. Alga, flags; consisting of plants whose root, leaf, and stem, are all one. Under this description are comprehended all the sea weeds, and some other aquatic plants.

58. Fungi, mushrooms. These plants are rarely branched, sometimes creeping, but most commonly erect. Such as are furnished with branches have them of a light spongy substance like cork. Mushrooms differ from the fuci, in that those which, like the fuci, have their seeds contained in capsules, are not branched, as that numerous class of sea weeds are. The greatest part of mushrooms have no root: some, instead

of roots, have a number of fibres, which, by their infuscations, frequently form a net with unequal meshes, some of which produce plants similar to their parent vegetable. The stamina in these plants are still undetermined. The seeds are spread over the surface of the plant, or placed in open holes or cavities, resembling the open capsules of some of the fuci. In mushrooms which are branched, the seeds are frequently visible by the naked eye, and always to be distinctly observed by the assistance of a good microscope. These plants are very astringent, and some of them are used for stopping violent hemorrhages. As a vegetable food, they are at best suspicious: some of them are rank poison.

Dubii ordinis. Under this name Linnæus classes all the other genera which cannot be reduced to any of the abovementioned orders, and which are near 120 in number.

ALPHABETICAL EXPLANATION OF BOTANICAL TERMS.

A.

Abrupt (abruptus), when a winged leaf ends without a little leaf.
Acorn, the seed of the oak.
Acotyledones, seeds without lobes, which produce no seminal leaves.
Acute (acutus), tapering to a slender, but not thorny termination.
Air-bag (folliculus), a distended seed-vessel opening on one side.
Alternate (alternus), applied to branches, leaves, or flowers, springing out regularly one above another.
Androgynous, having some flowers on the same plant, bearing stamens, but no pistils; and other flowers bearing pistils, but no stamens.
Angiosperma, seeds in a capsule.
Angular (angulatus) having corners; opposed to cylindrical.
— capsule, as in flower-de-luce.
Annual (annuus), living only one year.
Anomalous (anomalous), irregular.
Anther (anthera), the top of the stamen fixed on the filament, and containing the pollen.
Apex, termination.
Apophysis, excrescence.
Approaching, see Converging.
Arrow-shaped (agittatus), leaf shaped like an arrow head, as forrel.
Ascending, growing first horizontally, and then bent upwards.
Attenuate (attenuatus), tapering.
Avel-shaped (subulatus), slender.
Awn (arista) or beard, the sharp substance growing to the valves of corn or grass.
Awnless (muticus), without awns.
Axillary (axillaris) at the base of the leaves or branches.

B.

Bark (cortex), the covering of the stems, roots, and branches of vegetables.
Barren (masculi, abortivi), flowers or florets, which produce no perfect seeds; generally wanting pistils.

Base, see Axillary.
Battledore-shaped (spatulatum), rounded with a neck.
Beaded (granulatus), consisting of many little knobs, connected by small strings.
Beak or Bill (rostrum), a projecting appendage to some seeds.
Bearded (barbatus), beset with straight parallel hairs.
Bell-shaped (campanulatus), like the blossom of the convolvulus.
Bellying (ventricosus) distended.
Beneath (inferus), A blossom is beneath, when it includes the germ, and is attached to the part immediately below it. A germ is said to be beneath when it is not included in the corolla or blossom.
Berry (bacca), a pulpy seed-vessel without valves.
Biennial, continuing alive for two years.
Bird-footed (pedatus), resembling the foot of a land fowl.
Bitten, ending in no regular form, not tapering.
Bill (rostrum), a long substance attached to a seed.
Bladders, air-bags on some species of fucus.
Bladder-shaped (inflatus), inflated or distended.
Blistered, when the surface of a leaf rises high above the veins.
Blossom (corolla), part of a flower, consisting of one or more leaves called petals. It is an expansion of the inner bark of the plant, and is the ornamented cover of the stamens and pistils.
Blunt (obtusus), opposed to acute.
Boat-shaped (navicularis), like a little keel-bottomed boat.
Border (limbus), the upper part of a corolla of one petal.
Bow-ed (arcuatus), bent.
Branched (ramosus), having lateral divisions.
Bristles (setae), stiff cylindrical hairs.
Bristle-shaped (setaceus), slender, and like a bristle.
Bud (gemma), a protuberance on the stem or branches, containing the rudiments of the leaves or flowers to be expanded the following year.
Bulb (bulbus), a bud placed on the root or stem.
Bulging (gibbus), irregularly swollen out.

Bunch (racemus), a fruitstalk with short lateral branches.
Bundle (fasciculus), several flowers rising to the same point from their respective fruitstalks, as in sweet-william.
Bundled, applied to leaves, as in the larch.
Butterfly-shaped (papilionaceous), as the pea and broom flowers.

C.

Calycled (calyculatus), having a double calyx.
Calyptra, veil.
Calyx, or empalement, a continuation of the outer bark of the plant, forming part of the flower, is called a cup, as in primrose, involucre in carrot, catkin in hazel, calyptra in the mosses, a husk in oats, a sheath in the narcissus, and a curtain in some fungi.
Capsule, a dry hollow seed-vessel opening in some determinate manner.
Catkin (amentum), a composition of flowers and chaff on a long thread-shaped receptacle; the whole like a cat's tail; e.g. the willow.
Cell (loculamentum), vacuity in the capsule to lodge the seed.
Central florets (flores flosculosi); those which occupy the middle part of a compound flower, as the yellow ones in a common daisy.
Chaff (palea), thin membranaceous substance separating the florets from each other, and growing on a common receptacle.
Channelled (canaliculatus), having a furrow from the base to the end.
Cicatrized, scarred.
Ciliate, fringed.
Circumference, the florets farthest from the centre of a compound flower, as the white ones in the common daisy.
Clammy (viscosus), adhesive like birdlime.
Claw (unguis), the part of a petal next the base, distinguished from the limb or upper part.
Cleft, divided half-way down.
Climbing (scandens), plants that rise by the aid of others.
Clothing (pubes), hairiness on the surface of plants.
Cloven, see Cleft.
Club-shaped (clavatus), thinner at the base, and thicker upwards.
Cluster (thyrsus), flowers collected in somewhat of an egg-shaped form like the lilac.
Coated (tunicatus) root, composed of layers like the onion.
Cobwebbed (arachnoideus), covered with a substance resembling a cobweb.
Coloured (coloratus), not green.
Column (columella), the little pillar in the centre of some capsules to which the seeds are fixed.
Comb (coma), a collection of floral leaves terminating the flowering stem, as in the pine-apple.
Comb-like (pectinatum), a sort of winged leaf, the leaflets of which are like the teeth of a comb.
Common calyx, including several flowers, as in thistles.
Compact, growing close together.
Compound flowers (compositi flores) consist of many florets or little flowers, on a receptacle or seat, as in thistles.

VOL. IV. Part I.

Compressed (compressus), a cylindrical substance, more or less flattened.
Cone (strobilus), a seed-vessel, formed by a catkin, with hardened scales, as in the pine and fir.
Cone-shaped (cucullatus), applied to leaves rolled up, as the grocers roll up paper to hold spices, sugar, &c.
Confluent (confluentia folia), running into one another at the base.
Congregated (glomeratus), spikes, &c. crowded together, somewhat in a globular form.
Connected (adnatus) leaves, having their upper surface at the base, growing to the stem or branch.
Contiguous (adpressus) leaves, &c. close to the stem.
Converging (connivens), approaching at the top.
Convex, rising like the surface of a globe.
Convolute, twisted spirally.
Corolla, blossom.
Corymb, a collection of flowers standing each on its own fruitstalk, with fruitstalks of such a length, that the whole collection forms a flat broad surface at the top, as in the pear-tree.
Cotton, cottony (tomentum, tomentosus), downy, covered with a whitish soft substance.
Cotyledones, seed-lobes.
Creeping (repens) stem; creeping along the ground, as ivy.
— root, as spearmint.
Crescent-shaped (lunaris, lunatus), shaped like a waxing moon, like the anthers of the strawberry.
Crested (cristatus) flowers, tufted.
Crooked (cernuus) fruitstalk, with the flower facing the earth, and so stiff, that it cannot be straightened without breaking; as in the crown imperial.
Croft-pairs (decussatus), leaves in pairs, each pair pointing in a direction different from the pair above or below it.
Croft-shaped (cruciatus, cruciformis) flowers, having four petals in form of a cross; called cruciform plants.
Crowned (coronatus) seed, a seed to which the calyx adheres, ex gr. teazel.
Cruciform, see Croft-shaped.
Cryptogamia, stamens and pistils indistinct.
Cup (perianthium), a calyx contiguous to the other parts of the flower.
— double (calyculatus), when the base of one cup is surrounded by another cup.
Curtain (volva), the calyx of agarics and boleti.
Cut-round (circumscissus), when a seed-vessel opens in a circle, and not longways.
Cylindrical (teres), round like a walking stick.

D.

Dagger-pointed (mucronatus), ending, not gradually, but suddenly in a sharp point.
Deciduous (deciduus) leaves, which fall at the approach of winter.
— cup or calyx, falling off before the blossom.
— seed-vessel, falling off before it opens.
Declining (declinatus), bent like a bow, with the arch downwards.
Decurrent (decurrens) leaf, without a leaf-stalk, but where the leaf runs down the stem.

S f
Dented

Dented (retusus), a blunt leaf with a blunt notch at the end.

Depressed (depressus), when the surface of a leaf is slightly sunk.

Diadelphia, the 17th class of plants, having the filaments united into two sets.

Diamond-shaped (rhombeus), applied to leaves resembling a diamond on cards.

Diandra, two stamens.

Didynamia, the 14th class, having two stamens longer.

Dixynia, two pistils.

Dimpled (umbilicatus), having a hollow dot.

Dixyia, ditaceous, having the flowers bearing stamens and those bearing pistils on different plants.

Disk (discus) of a leaf is its surface; of a compound flower is its central florets; thus, in a daisy, the minute yellow florets form the disk, and the larger white strap-shaped florets form the ray.

Distant (distans), far asunder, as the stamens of mint.

Distended (ventricosus), as the cup of the rose.

Diverging (divergens), spreading wide from the stem almost horizontally, opposed to compact.

Divided (partitus) or parted, partite, signifies, that a cup, leaf, or petal, is parted more than half way down.

Dodecandra, 12 stamens.

Dorsal, fixed to the back.

Dotted (punctatus), marked with little hollow dots.

Double (didymus), applied to two anthers on one filament.

Doubly-compound (decompositus) leaves, having the primary leaf-stalk divided, so that each division forms a compound leaf. They are twinfork (bigeminus), when a forked leaf-stalk has several leaflets at the end of each fork; or, doubly-threefold (bi-ternatus), when a leaf-stalk, with three divisions, has three leaflets on the end of each division or fork; or, doubly-winged (duplicate-pinnatum, bi-pinnatum), when a leaf-stalk has lateral ribs, each of which forms a winged leaf.

Down (pappus), the fine hair or feather-like substance, crowning the seeds of some plants, and wafting them abroad.

Downy leaf, see Cottony.

Drupe, a pulpy seed-vessel, without valves, consisting of a nut, surrounded by a pulpy substance, ex gr. a cherry.

Dusted (pulveratus), applied to some plants which appear covered with a kind of powder.

E.

Ear-shaped (auriculatus), somewhat resembling a human ear.

Egg-shaped (ovatus), in form of an egg.

Egg-spear-shaped (ovato-lanceolatum), see Spear-egg-shaped.

Elliptic, oval.

Embracing (amplexicaulis) the stem, when the base of a leaf nearly surrounds the stem.

Entire (integer), opposed to cleft, gashed, &c.

Equal (æqualis), regular.

Erect, upright.

Even (lævis) surface; level, regular.

Excrecence (apophysis), a substance growing on some of the mosses from the seat of the flower.

Expanding (patens), between upright and horizontal.

Eye (hilum), the scar by which a seed is fixed to the seed-vessel.

F.

Feathered (plumosum), the down of seeds when it sends out lateral hairs.

Feeble (debilis), unable to stand upright.

Female flowers or florets, such as have one or more pistils, but no stamens.

Ferns, the 1st order of the class of cryptogamia.

Fertile (fertiles vel feminei) flowers, those that produce seed capable of vegetation. Those that have stamens only are always barren. Those that have pistils only, are only barren when placed beyond the reach of the pollen, coming from the anthers of staminiferous flowers.

Fibres (nervi), woody strings running along a leaf.

Fibrous (fibrosus) roots, composed of small threads or fibres.

Fiddle-shaped (panduriformis), oblong, but narrow in the middle and broader below.

Filament or thread (filamentum), the slender part of a stamen supporting the anther.

Fistulous, hollow.

Finger-like (digitatus) leaves, expanded like a man's hand, in pairs, threes and fives.

Flat-topped (saflagiatus), rising to the same height so as to form a flat surface.

Fleshy seed-vessel, ex gr. an apple.

Floral leaves (bractea) are generally on the fruitstalk, so that they are sometimes mistaken for the calyx; but the calyx withers when the fruit is ripe, whereas the floral leaves endure as long as the other leaves of the plant.

Floret (stosculus), one of the small flowers forming a compound or incorporated flower.

Flower (flos), a temporary part of a plant subservient to the formation of the seed. It consists of eight parts, a calyx, corolla or blossom, stamens, pistils, seed-vessel, seeds, receptacle, and a nectary. Wanting any of these parts, a flower is incomplete.

Forked (furcatus, dichotomus) divided.

Fringed (aliatus), as the blossom of the buck-bean.

Fruit (fructus), a part of a flower consisting of the seed-vessel, seeds, and receptacle.

Fruitstalk (pedunculus), a part of a branch or stem bearing flowers, but not leaves.

Fungus, the last order of the class of cryptogamia.

Funnel-shaped (infundibuliformis), when a one-petaled blossom is tubular at the base, and conical at the top.

Furrowed (fulcatus), marked with lines running lengthwise.

G.

Gaping (ringens, perfonatus) blossom, so called from its resemblance to a gaping mouth.

Gashed (lobatus), divided nearly half-way down into lobes, convex at the edges, and distant from each other.

Gelatinous, jelly-like.

Gem, bud.

General involucre, a calyx at the base of a general umbel, as in carrot.

Germ, germin, seed-bud.

Gibbous, bulged or bulging.

Gills (lamellæ), thin plates on the under side of the pileus.

Pileus or hat of an agaric; remarkable in the common mushroom.

Glast-shaped (cyathiformis), tubular, but widening towards the mouth.

Glaucous (glaucus), hoary, as the back of a cabbage-leaf.

Globular (globosus), like a round ball.

Glume (gluma), husk.

Gnawed (erofum), when an indented leaf seems as if bitten at the edges.

Granulated, resembling beads.

Gymnospermia, naked seeds.

Gynandria, stamens on the pistils.

H.

Hair-like (capillaris), slender, undivided, and cylindrical.

Hairs (pili), by some thought secretory ducts.

Hand-shaped (palmatus), like the human hand with the fingers expanded.

Hat (pileus), the upper broad part of fungusses.

Hatched-shaped (dolabriforme) leaf, like an axe of unequal thickness.

Headed (capitulus) stalk, supporting one compact knob of flowers at its extremity.

Heads (capitatus) of flowers, growing in compact knobs, as in peppermint.

Heart (corculum), that part of a seed which is the future plant in miniature.

Helmet (galea), the upper part of a gaping blossom.

Hemispherical, like a half globe.

Herbaceous stem, succulent, opposed to woody.

Hermaphrodite flowers or florets, such as contain both a stamen or stamens, and a pistil or pistils, as the greater part of flowers.

Hexagonal, or six-sided.

Hexogynia, having six pistils.

Hexandria, six-stamened.

Hoary (incanus), covered with a silvery-looking substance.

Honey-combed (favosum), a receptacle with cells open at the top, and having a seed in each.

Husk (gluma), the calyx of a grass plant.

Hybrid (hybrida), a plant produced by the pollen of one plant fertilizing the germ of a plant of a different species. A mule.

J.

Jagged (lacinatus) leaves, irregularly divided and subdivided into lobes.

Islandria, 20-stamened.

Imperfect flowers, wanting anther or pistil, or both.

Incomplete flowers want the cup, the corolla, or some other important part. See FLOWER. The tulip wants a cup, and the nettle has no blossom or corolla.

Incorporated (aggregatus), when little flowers or florets form a compound flower, as a daisy.

Incumbent, anthers fixed by the side, or stamens leaning or resting against.

Indented leaf, having the edges deeply scolloped, and the lobes far asunder.

Inflated, as if blown up like a bladder.

Inferior, see Beneath.

Interrupted, broken in its regular form, as a spike by leaves intervening.

Inversely heart-shaped (obcordatus), with the point of the heart next the stem.

Involucellum, a partial involucrum.

Involucrum or fence, the calyx of an umbel placed at a distance from the flowers.

Jointed (articulatus), ex gr. a wheat straw.

K.

Keel, the lowest petal in a butterfly-shaped corolla.

Keeled (carinatus), bent.

Knob, see HEAD.

L.

Lacteiferent, having a milky juice.

Laminated, when the flat surfaces of leaves lie close on each other.

Lateral branches, growing from the sides of the stem, opposed to terminating.

Leaf, the part of a plant corresponding to the lungs, and also to the organs of motion of animals.

Leaflet, or little leaf, one of the single leaves of a compound leaf.

Leaf-stalk (petiolus), the footstalk of a leaf.

Leather-like (coriaceus), tough-like leather.

Legume (legumen) or thell, a seed-vessel of two valves with the seeds fixed to one seam, as in the pea.

Level, when several branches or fruitstalks grow to equal heights.

Lid (operculum), a cover to the capsules of several mollus.

Ligulate, strap-shaped.

Limb, the upper spreading part of a petal.

Limber (flaccidus) bending with its own weight.

Lip, the upper or under division of a gaping corolla.

Lobes, the divisions of a gashed leaf.

Lopped (truncatus), looking as if cut off by scissors.

M.

Male flowers, which have stamens, but no pistils.

Matted (caespitosus), thickly interwoven.

Membranous, membranaceous, thin, skinny, and semi-transparent.

Monadelphia, united filaments.

Monandria, one stamen.

Monocasia, one house, or plants having the stamens and pistils in different flowers, but on the same plant.

Monogynia, one pistil in each flower.

Monopetalous, one-petaled.

Mouth (faux), the upper or opening part of the tube in a one-petaled corolla.

Mules, see HYBRID.

N.

Naked, without leaves or hairs.

Nectar, or honey-cup, the part of the flower which secretes the honey.

Nut, a seed covered by a hard woody shell.

O.

Oblong-egg-shaped, oblong at the base, but egg-shaped towards the end.

Oligandria, eight-stamened.

Oligogynia, eight pistils.

P.

Paleaceous, chaffy.

Panicle, an irregular assemblage of flowers.

Papilionaceous flowers, butterfly-shaped, as the pea.

Parasitical plants, which grow not on the earth, but on other plants.

Pedicle, a little fruitstalk.

Pentagon, five-cornered.

Pentagynia, five pistils.

S f 2

Pentandria,

Pentandrio, five-stamined.
Perennial, continuing more than two years.
Petals (petala), the leaves which constitute the corolla or blossom.
Pillar (stipes), the pedicle of the down of some seeds, as in dandelion.
Pimpled (papillosus), beset with little hard protuberances.
Pistil, a part of a flower consisting of the germen, the style, and the summit.
Pitcher-shaped (urceolatus), bellying like a jug.
Pith, a soft substance filling the cavity of some plants.
Plaited, folded.
Pod (siliqua), a seed-vessel of two valves, within which the seeds are alternately fixed to each seam.
Pollen (farina), a fine powder in the anthers of flowers.
Polyadelphia, stamens in three or more sets.
Polyandria, many stamens.
Polygamia, see Introduction to the 23d class.
Pores, little holes.
Pouch, a short pod.
Prism-shaped, different from cylindrical, in having the circumference angular.
Proliferous, when one flower, &c. springs out of another.
Pubescent, clothed with soft wool or hair.
R.
Radiate, compound flowers in which the florets of the centre differ from those of the circumference. See Dijk.
Rays (radii), outer florets of a radiate compound flower. See Dijk.
Receptacle, the seat or base of a flower.
Reflected, bent back.
Remote whorls, having a considerable length of stem between each.
Rhomboidal, nearly diamond-shaped.
Rigid, inflexible.
Root-leaves (radicalia), those which proceed immediately from the root.
Ruffle or ring, the part of the curtain of an agaric which adheres to the stem after the outer part is gone.
Runner (flagellum), a barren twig lying on the ground.
S.
Salver-shaped (hypocrateriformis), when a one-petaled corolla has its lower part tubular, and its higher part flat and expanded.
Scaled (squamosus), like the skin of a fish.
Scarify (squarrosus), applied to the rough cup of some compound flowers.
Seed, that which contains the rudiments of the new plant.
Seed-lobes (cotyledones), the perishable part of a seed which affords food to the rest in germination.

Seminal leaves, those which rise from the seed-lobes.
Serrated, like the teeth of a saw.
Sheath (spatha), a kind of calyx like that of the crocus.
Simple, undivided.
Sitting leaves or flowers, without leaf or flower-stalks.
Solitary, only one in a place.
Spear-egg-shaped, like a spear at the base, and an egg at the extremity.
Spike (spica), a collection of flowers placed alternately on each side of a common fruitstalk without little fruitstalks.
Stamen, consists of a filament and anther.
Staminiferous, bearing stamens.
Streaked, marked with depressed lines.
Syngensia, united anthers.
T.
Target-shaped leaf, having the stalk fixed, not in the edge, but the centre.
Tetragynia, four pistils.
Tetradynamia, four stamens longer.
Tiled, one leaf or scale partly covering another.
Tooth-ferrated, when the edge of a leaf is marked with little serrated teeth.
Triandria, three stamens.
Trigynia, three pistils.
Tubercled, having solid warts; applied to certain lichens.
Tuberous root, having many roundish knobs in a bundle.
Tubular, hollow like a tube.
U.
Umbel (umbella), an assemblage of flowers in which a number of slender fruitstalks, proceeding from the same centre, rise to nearly the same height, so as to form a regular surface at the top, as in hemlock or carrot.
Unequal florets (radiati), when the florets of an umbel are larger in the circumference than in the centre.
Valve, the pieces of a capsule are called valves.
Vaulted (fornicatus), like the roof of the mouth.
Veil (calyptra), the calyx of mosses.
W.
Wheel-shaped, a corolla of one petal, with a flat border and a short tube.
Whirls of branches, leaves or flowers; ex gr. the branches of the fir.
Wings, the lateral petals of a butterfly-shaped blossom.
Winged leaf-stalk, with a thin membrane on each side.
— leaf, when an undivided leaf-stalk has many little leaves growing from each side.
Wing cleft, a leaf deeply cut.
Woody, opposed to herbaceous.
Wool, curly haired clothing on some plants.
Z.
Zigzag, having many contrary bendings.

Zingiber Zernumbet.
Ginger. page 70.

BOTANY.
Monandria.

flower.

Boerhaavia scandens.
p. 77.

Plate XCIX.

Salicornia herbacea.
Jointed Gipswort. p. 77.

Diandria.

Calceolaria Nana.
p. 89.

Chionanthus Zeylanica.
Orange tree. p. 83.

Crantbemum parvifolium.
p. 83.

Aucubum Lucidum.
p. 93.

Piper Nigrum.
Black Pepper p. 93.

Abbott Prin. Nat. Sculptor, fecit.

This image shows a blank, aged page with a light beige background. There are faint, illegible text impressions scattered across the page, likely bleed-through from the reverse side. A large, faint botanical illustration of a plant with leaves and a central stem is visible, particularly in the lower half of the page. The paper shows signs of aging, including yellowing and some minor staining.

Cyperus Papyrus p. 101.

Wibrenia Maura p. 100.

A detailed botanical plate featuring three distinct plant species. On the left, Cyperus Papyrus is shown with its characteristic papyrus-like leaves and a large, feathery spikelet. In the center, Maspinia Sylvatica is depicted with a single, slender stem and a small, four-leaf whorl. On the right, Wibrenia Maura is illustrated with a cluster of narrow, linear leaves and a long, slender spikelet. The plants are shown with their root systems at the bottom, emerging from a common base. The illustration is rendered in a fine-line engraving style.

Maspinia Sylvatica.
p. 101.

A faint botanical illustration on aged paper, showing a central tree-like plant with a large, rounded, mossy or leafy canopy. To the left, there is a smaller plant with a fan-like arrangement of leaves. Below the central tree, there are several smaller plants, including one with a cluster of upright, pointed leaves and another with a more rounded, bushy form. The entire scene is rendered in a light, monochromatic style, possibly using pencil or light washes, and is framed by a thin border.

Ryegrass fulgo.

Purple Tussock Grass.

Soft Tussock Grass.

BOTANY. Triandra.

Bulbous Foxtail Grass.

Crooping Meadow Grass.

Plate CL.

Great Meadow Grass.

Time Bent Grass.

Crooping soft Grass.

Silver Hair Grass.

Temple Grass.

Crested Grass.

Droptail Grass.

Mijra.

Feather Grass.

Meadow Grass.

Foxtail Grass.

Mountain Hair Grass.

Annual Meadow Grass.

Water Spear Grass.

This is a botanical illustration plate, likely from a scientific work. It features a collection of plant specimens, possibly roots, stems, and leaves, arranged in a grid-like pattern. The specimens are rendered in a light, monochromatic style, possibly using pencil or light ink. The plants vary in size and shape, with some showing dense clusters of roots or leaves, and others showing more elongated, slender structures. The background is a plain, light-colored surface, and the overall appearance is that of an old, well-preserved scientific illustration.

Pretea argentea. p. 113.

A detailed botanical engraving of a branch from the Pretea argentea. The branch features several pairs of opposite, ovate leaves with finely serrated margins. At the top of the branch, there is a single flower with a large, rounded, and highly textured center, possibly representing the ovary or a cluster of stamens.

Samara Lota. p. 110.

An engraving of a branch from the Samara Lota. The branch has several pairs of opposite, ovate leaves with serrated edges. Attached to the branch is a long, cylindrical, and densely covered in small spines or bristles, which is likely a developing fruit or seed pod.

Banksia serrata. p. 113.

An engraving of a large, spherical fruit or seed pod from the Banksia serrata. The outer surface is covered in a dense layer of sharp, pointed spines. Inside the pod, several large, oval-shaped seeds or fruits are visible, each with a textured surface.

Dorstenia Contrayerva. p. 110.

An engraving of a plant from the Dorstenia Contrayerva. The plant has a cluster of deeply lobed, palmately divided leaves at its base. Several star-shaped structures, likely flowers or seed pods, are shown on long, thin stalks rising from the center of the plant. One of these structures is shown in detail, revealing a pattern of small dots or seeds.

Abel Prim. Wal. sculptor fecit.

A botanical illustration of a plant with pinnate leaves. The leaves are arranged in pairs along a central stem. A single, bright yellow flower is shown in full bloom, positioned centrally on the stem. The illustration is rendered in a light, muted greenish-yellow color.
A botanical illustration of a plant with pinnate leaves. The leaves are arranged in pairs along a central stem. A cluster of small, light-colored flowers is shown, positioned centrally on the stem. The illustration is rendered in a light, muted greenish-yellow color.
A botanical illustration of a plant with large, lobed leaves. The leaves are arranged in pairs along a central stem. The illustration is rendered in a light, muted greenish-yellow color.
A botanical illustration of a single, round fruit or seed pod. It is shown in a light, muted greenish-yellow color, with a small stem at the bottom.

Cinchona Officinalis

Jesuits Bark. p. 133.

Tripenunia Lappenica.
p. 130.

Cornus
Monspeliensis.
p. 137.

Abbott, Prin. Wal. & sculptor fecit.

This is a botanical illustration, likely a woodcut or engraving, rendered in a very light, muted greenish-grey ink on aged, yellowish paper. The central subject is a plant branch with several large, ovate leaves that have prominent veins. At the top of the branch is a terminal inflorescence, which appears to be a spike or raceme of small flowers or buds. To the left of the main branch, there is a smaller, more delicate plant with feathery, needle-like leaves. To the right, there are several small, rounded structures, possibly fruits or seed pods. The paper shows signs of age, including a vertical crease on the right side and some minor foxing or staining.

Oryza Sativa.
Rice plant. p. 160.

Tacca pinnatifida.
p. 159.

A detailed botanical illustration of Tacca pinnatifida. The main drawing shows a tall, slender stem with a cluster of long, narrow, pinnately divided leaves at the top. The leaves are deeply lobed and have a wavy, almost drooping appearance. At the base of the stem, there is a small, fibrous root system. To the right of the main plant, there is a separate, smaller illustration of a single, long, narrow leaf, showing its structure and the way it curves slightly at the tip.
A detailed botanical illustration of Oryza Sativa. It shows a cluster of rice panicles (ears) attached to a central stem. Each panicle is composed of numerous individual spikelets, which are small, oval-shaped grains. The spikelets are arranged in a dense, branched structure. The entire cluster is supported by a network of fine, root-like fibers.

Trillium sesile.
Three leaved Night Shade p. 161.

A detailed botanical illustration of Trillium sesile. It shows a single, upright stem with a cluster of three large, ovate leaves at the top. The leaves have prominent veins and a slightly serrated margin. At the base of the stem, there is a small, fibrous root system. The illustration is rendered in a fine-line style, showing the texture of the leaves and the structure of the stem.
A botanical illustration featuring three distinct plant specimens. The top-left plant has a dense, upright cluster of small, yellow flowers. The bottom-left plant has a single, large, trifoliate leaf with a long petiole and a small yellow flower. The rightmost plant is a taller, more complex specimen with multiple long, narrow, lanceolate leaves and a cluster of yellow flowers at its apex. The background is a light, aged paper with some faint, illegible text and small brown spots.

Tricentalis Europaea.
Chickweed winter green, p. 162.

Petiveria alliacea.
Guinea Henweed, p. 162.

Saururus Cernuus.
Lizards tail, p. 162.

Septis Capensis p. 162.

A botanical illustration of a plant with large, rounded, heart-shaped leaves. The leaves are arranged along a thin, slightly curved stem. Small, delicate flowers or buds are visible at the tips of the stem and between the leaves. The illustration is rendered in a light, muted greenish-grey tone.
A botanical illustration of a plant with a single, large, palmately lobed leaf. The leaf has several distinct lobes radiating from a central point. Below the leaf, a long, thin stem extends downwards to a fibrous root system. The illustration is rendered in a light, muted greenish-grey tone.
A botanical illustration of a plant with large, heart-shaped leaves. The leaves are arranged along a thin, slightly curved stem. Small, delicate flowers or buds are visible at the tips of the stem and between the leaves. The illustration is rendered in a light, muted greenish-grey tone.

Michelia Coriacea.

p. 107.

A detailed botanical engraving of a branch from Michelia Coriacea. The branch features several pairs of opposite, ovate leaves with serrated margins. At the top of the branch, there is a cluster of flowers, some of which are shown in full bloom and others as buds. The flowers have prominent, pointed stamens.

BOTANY.
octandria.

Plate CVI.

Ruginervilla Spectabilis.

A botanical engraving of a branch from Ruginervilla Spectabilis. The branch has large, ovate leaves with serrated margins and prominent veins. At the top, there is a large, terminal cluster of flowers. The flowers are shown in various stages of development, from buds to open flowers.

Michelia Campumlevis.

p. 107.

A botanical engraving of a branch from Michelia Campumlevis. The branch has large, lobed leaves with serrated margins. Several large, tubular flowers are shown, some in full bloom and others as buds. The flowers have prominent, pointed stamens.

Chelera Perfoliata.
Yellow Century p. 107.

A botanical engraving of a branch from Chelera Perfoliata. The branch has small, opposite, ovate leaves. At the top, there is a cluster of small flowers. The flowers are shown in various stages of development, from buds to open flowers.

Abott Prins. Wal. Sculptor. fecit.

A botanical illustration of a plant with large, rounded, ovate leaves. The leaves are arranged in opposite pairs along a central stem. At the top of the stem, there is a cluster of several large, rounded flowers or buds. The illustration is rendered in a light, muted greenish-grey tone.
A botanical illustration of a plant with pinnate leaves. The leaves are arranged in pairs along a central stem, with each pair consisting of two smaller leaflets. At the top of the stem, there is a terminal cluster of small, pointed flowers or buds. The illustration is rendered in a light, muted greenish-grey tone.
A botanical illustration of a plant with a single, large, bell-shaped flower. The flower is attached to a long, slender stem that has several small, pointed leaves. The illustration is rendered in a light, muted greenish-grey tone.
A botanical illustration of a plant with several flowers and leaves. The flowers are arranged along a central stem, with some flowers being more open and others more closed. The leaves are small and pointed, arranged along the stem. The illustration is rendered in a light, muted greenish-grey tone.

BOTANY.
Enneandria.

Plate CVII.

Caspyta Filiformis. p. 175.

Anacardium Occidentale.
Cashew nut tree. p. 175.

Butomus Umbellatus.
Flowering rush. p. 176.

Abott Prin. Wal. Sculptor. fecit.

A faint botanical illustration of a plant, possibly a dandelion, rendered in a light, sketchy style. The plant features a central stem with several large, rounded, heart-shaped leaves at the base. Above the leaves, a cluster of small, delicate flowers or seed heads is visible. The illustration is set against a light, aged background with some minor discoloration and faint rectangular outlines, suggesting it might be a page from an old book or a botanical plate.

Hematosylon Campechianum.
Legwood tree. p. 183.

A detailed botanical illustration of a branch from a Legwood tree. The branch features several trifoliate leaves with three ovate leaflets. At the tips of the branches are long, slender racemes bearing numerous small, tubular flowers.

Dionaea Muscipula.
Venus' Fly-trap. p. 183.

A botanical illustration of a Venus' Fly-trap. The main part of the image shows the plant's structure, including a central stem, several large, heart-shaped leaves with ciliate margins, and a flower at the top. To the left, there is a magnified view of the trap mechanism, showing the hinged lid and the sticky, glandular hairs on the inner surface of the trap.

Cherleria sedoides.
p. 186.

A botanical illustration of a dense, bushy plant. The leaves are very fine, needle-like, and arranged in a whorled pattern along the stems, giving the plant a feathery appearance.

Dentzia Scabra.
p. 186.

A botanical illustration of a branch from Dentzia Scabra. The leaves are ovate with serrated margins. The branch has several clusters of small, round fruits or seed pods.

Orchis Aedesella.
Woodswallow. p. 187.

A botanical illustration of a plant with a trifoliate leaf and a flower. To the right, there is a magnified view of the flower's structure, showing the petals and the central reproductive parts.
A botanical illustration of a plant with a central stem and several large, rounded leaves at the base. The stem has several small, rounded structures at the top, possibly buds or flowers.
A botanical illustration of a plant with a central stem and several smaller, more rounded leaves. The stem has several small, rounded structures at the top, possibly buds or flowers.
A botanical illustration of a plant with several stems and leaves. Some leaves are heart-shaped, and there are small, rounded structures at the top of some stems.
A botanical illustration of a plant with a central stem and several smaller, more rounded leaves. The stem has several small, rounded structures at the top, possibly buds or flowers.

Camellia Alta.
White Cinnamon p. 190.

A detailed botanical engraving of a Camellia Alta branch. The branch features several large, ovate leaves with prominent midribs and serrated margins. At the tips of the branches are clusters of small, round, dark berries. The central stem is straight and divides into several smaller branches bearing leaves and fruit.

Halesia Snowdrop Tree p. 190.

A botanical engraving of a Halesia branch. It shows a slender stem with several small, lanceolate leaves. Hanging from the stem are numerous small, bell-shaped flowers, each with a distinct calyx and a long, drooping style. The flowers are shown in various stages of development, from buds to fully opened flowers.

Asarum Europaeum p. 190.

A botanical engraving of an Asarum Europaeum branch. It features two large, heart-shaped (cordate) leaves with deeply lobed margins and prominent venation. Below the leaves, a small, developing fruit is shown, which is a small, rounded structure with a pointed tip and a persistent style.

Woll Prin. Wal. Sulphur. p. 190.

A faint, monochromatic botanical illustration of a plant, possibly a fern or a similar leafy plant, rendered in a light, muted green or grey tone. The plant features a central vertical stem with several large, rounded, heart-shaped leaves branching out. The illustration is set within a rectangular frame on a light-colored, slightly textured background. The overall appearance is that of a faded or watercolor-style print.

BOTANY.
Dodecaudria.

Garcinia Mangostana.
Mangosteen Tree.

Plate CX.

Fruit cut transversely.

Abbot's Grin Water, Sculpin, fruit.

PL. 1011

ROSE

ROSE

ROSE

ROSE

A botanical illustration of a rose branch. The central stem is vertical and has several pairs of leaves. The leaves are ovate with serrated margins and prominent pinnate venation. At the top of the stem, there is a small, unopened flower bud. On the left side of the stem, there is a cluster of three rose hips, which are the mature, fleshy receptacles of the rose flower. On the right side, there is a single rose hip. At the bottom of the stem, there is a large, circular cross-section of a rose hip, showing the internal structure with seeds and the outer layers.

Myrtus Pimento.
Jamaica Pepper Tree. p. 104.

A detailed botanical engraving of a Myrtus Pimento branch. The branch features several large, ovate leaves with prominent venation. At the top of the branch is a terminal raceme of small, dark, spherical fruits. The illustration is rendered in fine lines with subtle shading to show texture and form.
A small, separate botanical illustration showing a cluster of four small, round fruits on a short stem. The label 'Fruit.' is positioned above this cluster.

Dryas octopetala. p. 199.

A botanical illustration of Dryas octopetala. It shows a plant with a cluster of deeply lobed, ovate leaves at the base. Two flowers are shown in detail, each with eight petals and a central cluster of stamens. A single, long, slender stem extends upwards, ending in a dense, feathery cluster of stamens.

Tormentilla Erecta.
Upright Tormentil. p. 199.

A botanical illustration of Tormentilla Erecta. It depicts a plant with a thick, horizontal, tuberous root system. The stems are upright and branched, bearing several large, deeply palmately lobed leaves. Small, five-petaled flowers are shown at the tips of the stems. The illustration is detailed, showing the texture of the leaves and the structure of the root.

Abbott Print. Wats. Sculptor. Lond.

This image shows a faint, light-colored botanical illustration on aged, yellowish paper. The central element is a large plant with broad, ovate leaves and a cluster of small flowers at the top. Below this main plant are several smaller, more delicate flowering branches with smaller leaves. The entire illustration is rendered in a soft, muted greenish-blue or greyish-green tone, making it appear very light and delicate. The paper itself has a slightly textured appearance with some minor discoloration and small dark spots, characteristic of old paper.

With Small Challopis fruit.

A faint botanical illustration of a plant, possibly a species of Prunus or similar. The central feature is a vertical stem with several large, ovate leaves that have rounded tips and serrated margins. At the base of the stem, there are clusters of small, round fruits or flowers. To the right of the main stem, there is a smaller, more delicate branch with several small, bell-shaped flowers hanging from it. The entire illustration is rendered in a very light, muted color, making it difficult to discern fine details. The background is a plain, off-white or light beige color, showing some signs of age and wear.

Wintera Aromatica.
Winter's Bark. p. 208.

A detailed botanical engraving of a branch of Wintera Aromatica. The branch is woody and has several large, ovate leaves with prominent veins. At the tips of the branches are clusters of small, rounded flower buds. The illustration is rendered in a classic 18th-century style with fine line work and cross-hatching for shading.
A series of small, detailed botanical drawings of the plant's reproductive and vegetative parts, arranged in two rows. The top row includes: 12. a single stamen; 11. a pistil; 10. a pistil; 9. a pistil; 8. a pistil; 7. a pistil; 6. a pistil; 5. a pistil; 4. a pistil. The bottom row includes: 3. a pistil; 2. a pistil; 1. a pistil; 1. a pistil; 1. a pistil; 1. a pistil; 1. a pistil. The drawings show the internal structure of the flowers, including stamens and pistils.

Abbot Prim. Walp. sculptor fecit.

PL. 100

100

100

A botanical illustration of a plant, possibly a species of Magnolia or similar, featuring a central stem with several large, ovate leaves. The leaves have prominent veins and serrated margins. The illustration is rendered in a muted, monochromatic style, likely a woodcut or engraving. The plant is shown in a full, bushy habit, with the leaves arranged in opposite pairs along the stem.

100

Ajuga Reptans.
Creeping Bugle. p. 215.

A detailed botanical engraving of Ajuga Reptans. The plant is shown with a creeping, horizontal root system. A single upright stem rises from the center, bearing several opposite, ovate leaves with serrated margins. At the top of the stem is a dense, terminal raceme of small, tubular flowers.

Perilla Ceymoides. p. 217.

A botanical engraving of Perilla Ceymoides. The plant features large, ovate leaves with prominent serrated margins. A tall, slender, upright stem rises from the base, densely covered with small, tubular flowers. The leaves are shown in a spread-out arrangement, and the stem is shown with several smaller, developing flower clusters.

Glechoma Hederacea.
Ground Ivy. p. 217.

A botanical engraving of Glechoma Hederacea. The plant is shown with a creeping, horizontal root system. A single upright stem rises from the center, bearing several opposite, ovate leaves with serrated margins. At the top of the stem is a dense, terminal raceme of small, tubular flowers.

Lamium Album.
White Dead Nettle. p. 217.

A botanical engraving of Lamium Album. The plant is shown with a single upright stem bearing several opposite, ovate leaves with serrated margins. At the top of the stem is a dense, terminal raceme of small, tubular flowers. A detailed view of a single flower is shown at the bottom right, revealing its structure.

This botanical illustration depicts a plant with large, broad, ovate leaves that have slightly serrated margins. The leaves are arranged alternately along a central stem. At the top of the plant is a tall, slender, spike-like inflorescence with numerous small, densely packed flowers or fruits. The plant is shown with its root system at the base. The illustration is rendered in a muted, greenish-grey color palette, characteristic of older botanical prints.

This botanical illustration shows a plant with smaller, more rounded, and somewhat heart-shaped leaves. The leaves are arranged in a more compact, branched structure compared to the first plant. The inflorescence is also branched and appears to be composed of many small, rounded structures, possibly fruits or flowers. The plant is shown with its root system at the base. The illustration is rendered in a muted, greenish-grey color palette, consistent with the first illustration.

Myagrum Perfoliatum.
Gold of Pleasure, p. 223.

BOTANY,
Tetradynamia.

Hakile Maritima.
Sea Rocket, p. 223.

A detailed botanical drawing of a plant branch. The stem is slender and has several large, ovate leaves with prominent veins. At the top of the stem, there are several clusters of small, five-petaled flowers arranged in a whorl-like pattern.

Vella Annua.
Spanish Cress, p. 223.

Anastatica Microchanta.
Rose of Jericho, p. 223.

A botanical drawing of a plant branch. The stem is relatively thick and has several small, narrow, lanceolate leaves. At the top, there are several clusters of small, tubular flowers.
A botanical drawing of a plant branch. The stem is slender and has several small, narrow, lanceolate leaves. At the top, there are several clusters of small, tubular flowers.
A botanical drawing of a plant branch. The stem is slender and has several large, ovate leaves with prominent veins. At the top, there are several clusters of small, five-petaled flowers. The root system is also shown, with several roots branching out from the base of the stem.

Abbott, Prin. and, Taylor, fecit.

A botanical illustration of a plant with a slender, upright stem. The leaves are finely divided into small, linear segments, resembling fern fronds. Small, light-colored flowers are clustered at the tips of the branches. The drawing is rendered in a light, muted greenish-grey ink.
A botanical illustration of a plant with a single, thick, upright stem. It features several large, broad, ovate leaves with prominent veins. Small, light-colored flowers are scattered along the upper part of the stem. The illustration is done in a light, muted greenish-grey ink.
A botanical illustration of a plant with a thick, bulbous, rounded root. The leaves are large, ovate, and arranged in opposite pairs along a central stem. The leaves have a slightly wavy margin. Small, light-colored flowers are visible at the top of the stem. The drawing is rendered in a light, muted greenish-grey ink.
A botanical illustration of a plant with a slender, upright stem. The leaves are finely divided into small, linear segments, similar to the first plant. Small, light-colored flowers are clustered at the tips of the branches. The drawing is rendered in a light, muted greenish-grey ink.

Terraria Pavonia, p. 231.

A detailed botanical engraving of a flower from the genus Pavonia. The flower has five petals, each marked with a dark, irregular spot. The center of the flower is filled with numerous stamens. The flower is shown on a stem with several lanceolate leaves.

Aionia Capensis, p. 232.

An engraving of a flowering branch from the genus Aionia. The branch is slender and bears several narrow, lanceolate leaves. At the top of the branch, there are two tubular flowers with prominent, protruding stamens.

Bombax Heptaphyllum.
Silk Cotton tree, p. 233.

An engraving of a branch from the Silk Cotton tree. The branch features a large, palmately compound leaf with seven leaflets. A smaller branch from the same plant is shown, bearing a cluster of small, tubular flowers.

Adansonia Digitata.
Sour Gourd, p. 233.

An engraving of a large, flat, lobed leaf from the Sour Gourd tree. The leaf has several broad, rounded lobes. A long, curved stem rises from the center of the leaf, terminating in a small flower.
A faint botanical illustration on aged, yellowish paper. The illustration depicts four distinct plant specimens. In the upper left, a tall, slender stem is shown with several small, lanceolate leaves. In the upper right, a plant with a more complex structure is visible, featuring a cluster of small flowers or buds at the top and several larger, rounded leaves. In the lower left, a large, broad, rounded leaf is shown, possibly a single leaf or a small cluster. In the lower right, a plant with a central stem and several large, pointed leaves is depicted. The entire illustration is rendered in a very light, muted green or greyish-green ink, making it appear almost like a watermark or a faded print. The paper itself shows signs of age, with some minor discoloration and small spots.

Monnieria Trifolia. p. 237.

BOTANY.
Diadelphia.

Plate C XVII.

Geoffroya Spinosa. Base Cabbage tree.
p. 240.

A detailed botanical engraving featuring four distinct plant specimens. The central figure is a large branch of Monnieria Trifolia, characterized by its bipinnate leaves and a dense, terminal raceme of small flowers. To the upper right, a single, smooth, oval-shaped fruit of Geoffroya Spinosa is depicted. In the lower left corner, a smaller branch of Arachis hypogaea is shown, featuring trifoliate leaves and small, round, developing pods. In the lower right corner, a branch of Bisferula Polecina is illustrated, showing its serrated leaves and a terminal raceme of small flowers. The entire illustration is rendered in a classic black and white engraving style with fine line work and cross-hatching for texture and shading.

Arachis Hypogaea.
Groundnut.
p. 238.

Bisferula Polecina.
Base Hutchet Vetch.
p. 240.

Adell. Prin. Wal. sculptor. fecit.

A faint botanical illustration of a plant, possibly a species of Eucalyptus or similar. The plant features large, lobed leaves and a central cluster of small, round fruits or flowers. The illustration is rendered in a light, muted greenish-grey tone, appearing as a watermark or a faded print on the aged, yellowish paper. The background shows signs of age, including small brown spots and a faint horizontal line near the top.

Citrus Medica. Forbidden fruit tree. p. 242.

A detailed botanical engraving of a Citrus Medica tree. The tree has a central trunk that branches out into several smaller limbs. The leaves are small, oval-shaped, and arranged in opposite pairs along the branches. Small clusters of flowers are visible at the tips of some branches. The tree is shown growing from a small mound of earth.

Monsonia speciosa.
p. 242.

Theobroma Cacao.
Cacao or Chocolate nut tree.
p. 242.

A detailed botanical engraving of a Theobroma Cacao tree. The illustration shows a thick, textured trunk with a large, ribbed, oval-shaped fruit hanging from a branch. The fruit has a distinct pattern of small, raised bumps. Next to the trunk is a large, single leaf with prominent veins and a serrated margin. Small flowers are also depicted on the branches.
A detailed botanical engraving of Monsonia speciosa. The illustration shows a plant with several large, heart-shaped leaves with serrated edges. A long, slender stem rises from the leaves, bearing a cluster of flowers at the top. The flowers have five distinct petals and a long, thin style.

Walt Peirce, Watercolorist.

A botanical illustration featuring a central tree with a slender trunk and a dense canopy of small, oval-shaped leaves. To the left of the tree is a flowering plant with several large, rounded blossoms and smaller leaves. To the right of the tree is a large, elongated, oval-shaped object, possibly a seed pod or a fruit, with a textured surface. The entire illustration is rendered in a light, muted greenish-grey tone on a cream-colored background. There are some faint, illegible markings or bleed-through from the reverse side of the page visible in the background.

A botanical illustration of a plant with large, ovate leaves and several clusters of small flowers at the top of the stems. The plant has a central stem with several large, ovate leaves and several smaller, more distant flower clusters.
A botanical illustration of a plant with small, serrated leaves and a central stem. The plant has a central stem with several small, serrated leaves.
A botanical illustration of a plant with large, broad leaves and a central stem. The plant has a central stem with several large, broad leaves.
A botanical illustration of a flower head with many long, thin petals or stamens radiating from a central disk. The flower head is circular with many long, thin petals or stamens radiating from a central disk.
A botanical illustration of a plant with large, spiny leaves and a central stem. The plant has a central stem with several large, spiny leaves.
A botanical illustration of a flower head with many short, thick petals or stamens radiating from a central disk. The flower head is circular with many short, thick petals or stamens radiating from a central disk.

Walt. Prin. Wal. Sculptor. fecit.

A botanical illustration showing a cluster of three large, oval-shaped fruits or seed pods, each with a pointed tip. Above them is a single, large, star-shaped flower with numerous thin, radiating petals or stamens. The drawing is rendered in a light, monochromatic style, possibly a pencil or light wash illustration.
A botanical illustration of a plant branch with several large, ovate leaves. The leaves have prominent veins and pointed tips. At the top of the branch, there is a cluster of small, bell-shaped flowers or buds. The illustration is rendered in a light, monochromatic style, possibly a pencil or light wash illustration.
Artocarpus Incisa.
Bread fruit Tree. p. 266.
A detailed botanical engraving. The central figure is a branch of Artocarpus Incisa, featuring large, deeply lobed leaves and two large, round, textured fruits. To the left, there is a smaller plant with a dense, rounded habit and a root system, labeled Phyllanthus Alligatum. Below it is a small, separate botanical detail of a flower or fruit. To the right, a tall, slender spike of a plant is shown, labeled Tripsacum Hermaphroditum. At the bottom right, there is a single, long, narrow leaf. The artist's signature, 'A. Bell. Prin. Wal. Sculptor. fecit.', is at the bottom right.
Phyllanthus Alligatum.
Tripsacum Hermaphroditum.
p. 267.
A. Bell. Prin. Wal. Sculptor. fecit.
A botanical illustration of a plant, possibly a species of walnut or a similar tree. The central feature is a large, ovate leaf with prominent veins, from which several smaller, lanceolate leaves branch out. Below the leaves, a cluster of large, round fruits hangs from a central stem. The fruits are depicted with some shading to show their form. The entire illustration is enclosed within a faint, rectangular border. At the top of the illustration, there is some faint, illegible text, likely a title or reference. At the bottom, there is also some faint text, possibly a description or a reference to a specific part of the plant. The background is a light, off-white color, and the overall style is that of a classic botanical plate from a scientific publication.

Carica Papaya, Pajaro tree, Male, p. 274.

Ratis Maritima, p. 276.

Viscum Album, White Mistletoe, p. 276.

A detailed botanical engraving showing three distinct plants. In the center is a tall, slender tree with a thick trunk and large, deeply lobed leaves, identified as a male Papaya tree. To the left is a smaller plant with a vertical stem and small, oval leaves, identified as Ratis Maritima. To the right is a branch of White Mistletoe with small, oval leaves and clusters of small, round fruits. The plants are rendered in fine line work with some stippling for texture and shading.

A. Holl. Print. W. H. Schulpers fecit.

PL. 100. Quercus alba L.

A botanical illustration of a white oak (Quercus alba). The central figure is a full tree with a straight trunk and a dense, rounded canopy of leaves. To the left of the tree is a branch with several large, ovate leaves and a cluster of acorns. To the right is a single, upright acorn with its cap. The illustration is rendered in a light, monochromatic style, typical of 18th-century botanical prints.

Carica Papaya, Papaw Tree Female p. 274.

A detailed botanical engraving of a Papaw Tree (Carica Papaya). The tree has a tall, straight, textured trunk with a small cluster of roots at the base. The leaves are large, deeply lobed, and have serrated margins. Several large, oval-shaped fruits are shown hanging from the trunk and the leaf axils.

Antidesma Mexicana, Chinese Laurel.
p. 277.

A botanical illustration of Antidesma Mexicana, also known as the Chinese Laurel. It features a branch with several large, ovate leaves that have prominent veins. Two small, elongated clusters of small fruits or seed pods are shown on the branch.

Hydrocharis Morsus ranae.
Frog-bit. p. 277.

A botanical illustration of Hydrocharis Morsus ranae, commonly known as Frog-bit. It shows a cluster of heart-shaped leaves with a prominent central vein. Several small, five-petaled flowers are attached to the leaves, and a small root system is visible at the base.

Abott Prin. Wal. Souffler. fecit.

A detailed botanical illustration of a tall tree, possibly a species of maple or birch, with a thick, straight trunk and large, deeply lobed leaves. The tree is shown in full, with its canopy spreading out. The leaves are large and have a distinct, palmate or pinnate structure with many small lobes. The trunk is smooth and tapers slightly towards the top. The base of the tree is shown with some ground detail.
A botanical illustration of a plant with several large, round, heart-shaped leaves. The leaves are arranged in a cluster, with some showing small flowers or buds. The plant has a central stem and several smaller, thinner leaves. The overall appearance is that of a herbaceous plant with large, rounded leaves.
A botanical illustration of a plant with large, ovate leaves. The leaves are broad and have a slightly pointed tip. A flowering branch is shown, with several small flowers or buds. The plant has a central stem and several smaller, thinner leaves. The overall appearance is that of a herbaceous plant with large, ovate leaves.
Myristica Moschata. Nutmeg tree.

This botanical plate illustrates the Myristica Moschata, or Nutmeg tree. The central figure (a) shows a branch with several large, ovate leaves and two fruits. One fruit is whole, and the other is split open to reveal the internal structure. To the right, a detailed view (b) shows a cross-section of a fruit, revealing the internal chambers and the central nutmeg. Below this, several smaller illustrations show different parts of the fruit: (c) a single nutmeg, (d) a cross-section of a nutmeg, (e) a longitudinal section of a nutmeg, (f) a cross-section of a nutmeg, (g) a whole nutmeg, and (h) a whole nutmeg. To the left, a separate illustration (i) shows the 'Mace covering the Nutmeg', which is a complex, branched structure. The caption 'Different parts of the fruit.' is placed near the top right, and 'Mace covering the Nutmeg.' is placed near the bottom left. The caption 'Nutmeg.' is placed near the bottom right, and 'A Ball of mace that is the true fruit.' is placed at the bottom right.

Different parts of the fruit.
Mace covering the Nutmeg.
Nutmeg.
A Ball of mace that is the true fruit.
A faint botanical illustration on aged paper. The drawing depicts a central branch with several large, ovate leaves. Below the branch, there are several round, seed-like objects, some of which are enclosed in circular outlines. In the lower right corner, there is a faint, cross-shaped structure that may represent a flower or a seed pod. The overall style is that of a scientific plate or a page from a botanical manuscript.

Mimosa nilotica. p. 282.

A detailed botanical illustration of a branch from a Mimosa nilotica tree. The branch features several bipinnate leaves with small, oval leaflets. Interspersed among the leaves are several large, round, textured flower heads (spherical inflorescences) and smaller, less developed ones.

Mimosa Catalpa. p. 282.

A botanical illustration of a branch from a Mimosa Catalpa tree. The branch has bipinnate leaves with small, linear leaflets. A prominent, long, slender, cylindrical flower head (spike) is shown extending from the branch, with smaller, less developed ones nearby.

Mimosa cinerea. p. 282.

A botanical illustration of a branch from a Mimosa cinerea tree. The branch has bipinnate leaves with small, oval leaflets. Several long, slender, cylindrical flower heads (spikes) are shown extending from the branch, with smaller, less developed ones nearby.

Mimosa scandens.
p. 282.

A botanical illustration of a branch from a Mimosa scandens tree. The branch has bipinnate leaves with small, oval leaflets. A long, slender, cylindrical flower head (spike) is shown extending from the branch, with smaller, less developed ones nearby.

A detailed botanical illustration of a fern frond, showing the central rachis and several pairs of pinnae. The pinnae are pinnatifid, with the central vein clearly visible. The illustration is rendered in a light, monochromatic style.
A detailed botanical illustration of a fern frond, showing the central rachis and several pairs of pinnae. The pinnae are pinnatifid, with the central vein clearly visible. The illustration is rendered in a light, monochromatic style.
A detailed botanical illustration of a fern frond, showing the central rachis and several pairs of pinnae. The pinnae are pinnatifid, with the central vein clearly visible. The illustration is rendered in a light, monochromatic style.

A botanical illustration featuring two palm trees and a seed. The tree on the left is a tall, slender palm with a single trunk and a crown of long, narrow leaves. The tree on the right is a shorter, bushier palm with a more rounded crown of leaves. To the left of the trees, there is a small, oval-shaped seed with a textured surface. The illustration is rendered in a light, monochromatic style, possibly a woodcut or engraving, and is set against a plain, aged background.

Alga.

A detailed botanical illustration of an alga, showing several branches with small, rounded, scale-like structures, possibly representing a type of seaweed or a bryophyte branch.

Alnus.

A botanical illustration of an Alnus branch, showing a long, slender stem with small, pointed leaves and a terminal catkin.
A botanical illustration of a mossy or lichen-like structure, showing a dense, tufted growth on a substrate.
A botanical illustration of a mossy or lichen-like structure, showing a cylindrical, segmented growth form.

Hypnum.

A botanical illustration of a mossy structure, showing a small, tufted growth form.
A botanical illustration of a mossy branch, showing a branch with small, scale-like leaves.
A botanical illustration of a mossy plant, showing a complex, branched structure with small, scale-like leaves and several upright, stalk-like structures.

Sphagnum.

A botanical illustration of Sphagnum moss, showing several upright, branched stems with small, scale-like leaves.

Conferva.

A botanical illustration of a Conferva branch, showing a branch with small, scale-like leaves.
A botanical illustration of a Conferva plant, showing a long, slender, segmented stem and a small, tufted growth form.

Fontinalis.

A botanical illustration of Fontinalis moss, showing a complex, branched structure with small, scale-like leaves and a terminal structure.
A botanical illustration of a mossy branch, showing a branch with small, scale-like leaves.

Anthoceros, a small plant, showing a branch with small, scale-like leaves.

This is a botanical illustration, likely from a historical medical or scientific text. It depicts various plant specimens, including a long, thick root or rhizome, a cluster of fibrous roots, and several smaller, more delicate plant parts. The drawings are rendered in a light, monochromatic style, possibly using pencil or light ink. The background is a plain, aged paper with some minor discoloration and faint, illegible markings at the top.

COCOS NUCIFERA, The Cocoa Nut Tree.

A detailed botanical engraving of three palm trees. The central tree is the tallest, with a long, slender trunk and a crown of large, feathery fronds. A cluster of small, round fruits is shown growing from the base of the fronds. To its left is a shorter tree with a more textured trunk and a dense, rounded crown of smaller fronds. To its right is a shorter tree with a more segmented trunk and a crown of long, narrow, lanceolate fronds. All three trees are shown growing from a small patch of ground with some grass.

Phoenix Dactylifera, Date palm.

Areca Cereus, Cabbage tree.

A faint, light blue-green illustration of three palm trees on a small patch of ground. The central tree is the tallest, with its trunk rising from a small mound of earth. To its left and right are two shorter, bushier palm trees. The entire scene is rendered in a very light, almost monochromatic blue-green wash, giving it a ghostly or faded appearance. The background is a plain, off-white or light beige paper with some minor foxing or staining.

B O T
B O T

BOTANY-BAY, so called from the great number of new plants which were discovered by the naturalists who first visited it. See New Holland.

BOTANOMANCY, (from botanus, herb, and manus, divination), an ancient species of divination, by means of plants; especially sage and fig leaves. The manner of performing it was thus: the persons who consulted wrote their own names and their questions on leaves, which they exposed to the wind; and as many of the letters as remained in their own places were taken up, and being joined together, contained an answer to the question.

BOTARGO, a kind of faulage, made with the eggs and blood of the mullet, a large fish common in the Mediterranean. The best kind comes from Tunis in Barbary: It must be chosen dry and reddish. The people of Provence use a great deal of it, the common way of eating it being with olive oil and lemon juice. There is also a great consumption of it throughout the Levant.

BOTE, (Sax.), signifies a recompense, satisfaction, or amends: hence comes manbote, compensation or amends for a man slain, &c. In King Ina's laws is declared what rate was ordained for expiation of this offence, according to the quality of the person slain. From hence likewise we have our common phrase, to-bost, i. e. compensationis gratia. There are bouse-bote, plough-bote, &c. privileges to tenants in cutting of wood, &c.

BOTELESS, (fine remedio). In the charter of Hen. I. to Tho. archbishop of York, it is said, "that no judgment or sum of money, shall acquit him that commits sacrilege; but he is in English called boteless, viz. without emendation." We retain the word still in common speech: as, It is boteless to attempt such a thing; that is, it is in vain to attempt it.

BOTH, JOHN AND ANDREW, Flemish painters, and pupils of Bloemart. The union of these brothers was very singular; they were inseparable in their studies, travels, and paintings. John painted the landscape part of their pictures in the manner of Lorrain, and Andrew the figures and animals in the style of Bambocche. They both died in 1650. John's taste in landscape is elegant; his ideas are grand; his composition beautiful; and his execution rich and masterly in the highest degree. His light is not always well distributed; but his figures are excellent. It is to be regretted that we have not more of his works; for they are certainly, upon the whole, among the best landscapes we have.

BOTHNIA, a province of Sweden, at the end of

the gulf of the same name. It is divided into two parts called east and west Bothnia, the former of which belongs to Finland. West Bothnia is full of mountains; the earth is sandy, and yet a scarcity of provisions is seldom known. Cattle and game are so common, salmon and a sort of herrings so plenty, and the trade of skins is so gainful, that the inhabitants can command what they want from their neighbours. There are only two towns worth mentioning, viz. Tornea and Uma. The inhabitants of this province are Protestants; and are a civil well-behaved people.

BOTRYS, BOTRUS, or Bosra, in Ancient Geography, a town of Phoenicia, on the Mediterranean, built by Saturn, (a proof at least of antiquity); twelve miles to the north of Byblus, and twenty to the south of Tripolis. Now almost in ruins, and called Boteron, or Boturn, (Pofcellus). E. Long. 37. 30. N. Lat. 34. 6.

BOTT, among bone-lace weavers, a kind of round cushion of light matter placed on the knee, whereon they work or weave their lace with bobbins, &c.

BOTT, in Zoology. See Botts.

BOTTICELLI, SANDRO, or ALESSANDRO, born at Florence in 1437, learned the rudiments of painting under Filippo Lippi. He executed several pictures for Pope Sixtus IV. and others for the city of Florence: for these he received large sums of money, all of which he expended, and died at last in great distress, aged 78. He was not only a painter but a man of letters. Baldini, according to the general report, communicated to him the secret of engraving, then newly discovered by Finiguerra their townsman. The famous edition of Dante's Poem of Hell, printed at Florence by Nicholo Lorenzo della Magna, A. D. 1481, and to which, according to some authors, Botticelli undertook to write notes, was evidently intended to have been ornamented with prints, one for each canto; and these prints (as many of them as were finished) were designed, if not engraved, by Botticelli. It is remarkable, that the two first plates only were printed upon the leaves of the book, and for want of a blank space at the head of the first canto, the plate belonging to it is placed at the bottom of the page. Blank spaces are left for all the rest; that as many of them as were finished might be pasted on. Mr. Wilbraham possesses the finest copy of this book extant in any private library; and the number of prints in it amounts to nineteen. The two first, as usual, are printed on the leaves; and the other seventeen, which follow regularly, are pasted on the blank spaces; and these apparently were all that Botticelli ever executed.
